doc.question: In Chord, assume the size of the identifier space is 16. The active nodes are N3, N6, N8 and N12. Show all the target key (in ascending order, ignore the node's identifier itself) for N6.

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: In Chord, assume the size of the identifier space is 16. The active nodes are N3, N6, N8 and N12. Show all the target key (in ascending order, ignore the node's identifier itself) for N6.
A. [7, 10, 13, 14]
B. [7, 9, 12, 15]
C. [7, 8, 10, 14]
D. [7, 8, 12, 14]
E. [7, 9, 11, 13]
F. [6, 8, 10, 14]
G. [6, 7, 9, 11]
H. [8, 9, 11, 13]
I. [3, 6, 8, 12]
J. [8, 10, 12, 14]
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
In Chord each node n has a finger table with entries for the start keys  

\( (n + 2^{i-1}) \bmod 2^{m} \) for i = 1 … m, where m = log₂(identifier‑space).  

Here the identifier space is 16, so m = 4.  

For node N6:

- i = 1: \( (6 + 2^{0}) \bmod 16 = 7 \)  
- i = 2: \( (6 + 2^{1}) \bmod 16 = 8 \)  
- i = 3: \( (6 + 2^{2}) \bmod 16 = 10 \)  
- i = 4: \( (6 + 2^{3}) \bmod 16 = 14 \)

Ignoring the node’s own identifier (6), the target keys are **[7, 8, 10, 14]**.

Thus the correct choice is:

**C. [7, 8, 10, 14]**  

the answer is (C)

doc.question: Two single-user workstations are attached to the same local area network. On one of these workstations, file pages are accessed over the network from a file server; the average access time per page is 0.1 second. On the other of these workstations, file pages are accessed from a local disk; the average access time per page is 0.05 second. A particular compilation requires 30 seconds of computation and 200 file page accesses. What is the ratio of the total time required by this compilation if run on the diskless (file server) workstation to the total time required if run on the workstation with the local disk, if it is assumed that computation is not overlapped with file access?

On the other of these workstations, file pages are accessed from a local disk; the average access time per page is 0.05 second. A particular compilation requires 30 seconds of computation and 200 file page accesses. What is the ratio of the total time required by this compilation if run on the diskless (file server) workstation to the total time required if run on the workstation with the local disk, if it is assumed that computation is not overlapped with file access?
A. 5/4
B. 10/5
C. 6/5
D. 5/3
E. 3/1
F. 4/3
G. 1/1
H. 7/4
I. 3/2
J. 2/1
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The diskless workstation needs \(30\text{ s} + 200 \times 0.1\text{ s}=30+20=50\text{ s}\).  
The workstation with a local disk needs \(30\text{ s} + 200 \times 0.05\text{ s}=30+10=40\text{ s}\).  

The ratio is \(\dfrac{50}{40} = \dfrac{5}{4}\).

**The answer is (A).**

doc.question: What is the number of labelled forests on 8 vertices with 5 connected components, such that vertices 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 all belong to different connected components?

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: What is the number of labelled forests on 8 vertices with 5 connected components, such that vertices 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 all belong to different connected components?
A. 620
B. 520
C. 220
D. 120
E. 820
F. 320
G. 420
H. 920
I. 720
J. 280
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The number of labelled forests on \(n\) vertices with exactly \(k\) components in which a specified set of \(k\) vertices each lie in a different component is given by Cayley’s formula for rooted forests:

\[
\text{count}=k\; n^{\,n-k-1}.
\]

Here \(n=8\) and the specified roots are vertices \(1,2,3,4,5\), so \(k=5\).

\[
\text{count}=5 \times 8^{\,8-5-1}=5 \times 8^{2}=5 \times 64=320.
\]

Thus the required number of labelled forests is **320**, which corresponds to option **F**.

**the answer is (F)**

doc.question: To compute the matrix product M_1M_2, where M_1 has p rows and q columns and where M_2 has q rows and r columns, takes time proportional to pqr, and the result is a matrix of p rows and r columns. Consider the product of three matrices N_1N_2N_3 that have, respectively, w rows and x columns, x rows and y columns, and y rows and z columns. Under what condition will it take less time to compute the product as (N_1N_2)N_3 (i.e., multiply the first two matrices first) than to compute it as N_1(N_2 N_3)?
choices: There is no such condition; i.e., they will always take the same time.

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: To compute the matrix product M_1M_2, where M_1 has p rows and q columns and where M_2 has q rows and r columns, takes time proportional to pqr, and the result is a matrix of p rows and r columns. Consider the product of three matrices N_1N_2N_3 that have, respectively, w rows and x columns, x rows and y columns, and y rows and z columns. Under what condition will it take less time to compute the product as (N_1N_2)N_3 (i.e., multiply the first two matrices first) than to compute it as N_1(N_2 N_3)?
A. w > z
B. 1/w + 1/y > 1/x + 1/z
C. y > z
D. 1/x + 1/z < 1/w + 1/y
E. 1/w + 1/z > 1/x + 1/y
F. There is no such condition; i.e., they will always take the same time.
G. x > y
H. 1/w + 1/x < 1/y + 1/z
I. 1/x + 1/y > 1/w + 1/z
J. x < y
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The cost of \((N_1N_2)N_3\) is  

\[
\text{cost}_1 = wxy + wyz = wy(x+z)=wy\left(\frac{x}{z}+\frac{z}{x}\right)=wy\left(\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{z}\right) .
\]

The cost of \(N_1(N_2N_3)\) is  

\[
\text{cost}_2 = xyz + wxz = xz(y+w)=xz\left(\frac{y}{w}+\frac{w}{y}\right)=xz\left(\frac{1}{w}+\frac{1}{y}\right) .
\]

We need \(\text{cost}_1 < \text{cost}_2\):

\[
wy\left(\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{z}\right) < xz\left(\frac{1}{w}+\frac{1}{y}\right)
\]

Dividing by the positive product \(wxyz\) gives  

\[
\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{z} < \frac{1}{w}+\frac{1}{y}.
\]

Thus the product is faster when  

\[
\boxed{\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{z} < \frac{1}{w}+\frac{1}{y}}.
\]

This corresponds to option **D**.  

the answer is (D)

doc.question: At time 0, five jobs are available for execution on a single processor, with service times of 25, 15, 5, 3, and 2 time units. Which of the following is the minimum value of the average completion time of these jobs?

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: At time 0, five jobs are available for execution on a single processor, with service times of 25, 15, 5, 3, and 2 time units. Which of the following is the minimum value of the average completion time of these jobs?
A. 40
B. 10
C. 76/5
D. 100/5
E. 60
F. 208/5
G. 92/5
H. 30
I. 20
J. 50
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
To minimize the average completion time on a single processor when all jobs are available at time 0, we schedule them in order of shortest processing time first (SPT).

**Order of jobs (shortest to longest):** 2, 3, 5, 15, 25  

Compute completion times:
- After 2 units → completion = 2  
- After 2 + 3 = 5 units → completion = 5  
- After 5 + 5 = 10 units → completion = 10  
- After 10 + 15 = 25 units → completion = 25  
- After 25 + 25 = 50 units → completion = 50  

Sum of completion times = 2 + 5 + 10 + 25 + 50 = 92  

Average completion time = 92 / 5 = 92/5.

The option matching this value is **G**.

**the answer is (G)**

doc.question: Consider Convolutional Neural Network D2 which takes input images of size 32x32 with 1 colour channels. The first layer of D2 uses 4 filters of size 5x5, a stride of 2, and zero-padding of width 1. Consider CNN D2 which takes input images of size 32x32 with 1 colour channels. The first layer of D2 uses 4 filters of size 5x5, a stride of 2, and zero-padding of width 1. What is the total number of weights defined for the entire activation output of this first layer? (ie. If you flattened all filters and channels into a single vector)

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: Consider Convolutional Neural Network D2 which takes input images of size 32x32 with 1 colour channels. The first layer of D2 uses 4 filters of size 5x5, a stride of 2, and zero-padding of width 1. Consider CNN D2 which takes input images of size 32x32 with 1 colour channels. The first layer of D2 uses 4 filters of size 5x5, a stride of 2, and zero-padding of width 1. What is the total number of weights defined for the entire activation output of this first layer? (ie. If you flattened all filters and channels into a single vector)
A. 1200
B. 500
C. 1300
D. 1040
E. 1100
F. 600
G. 900
H. 1000
I. 700
J. 800
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The output of the first convolutional layer has dimensions  

\[
\text{output size} = \left\lfloor\frac{32 + 2\cdot1 - 5}{2}\right\rfloor + 1 = 15
\]

so each of the 4 filters produces a \(15 \times 15\) feature map.  
Total number of values (weights for the whole activation output) is  

\[
4 \times 15 \times 15 = 4 \times 225 = 900.
\]

Thus the correct choice is **G. 900**.  

the answer is (G)

doc.question: The following signal $x_1(t)=\cos (3 \pi t)-4 \cos (5 \pi t-0.5 \pi)$ can be expressed as $x_1(t)=\operatorname{Real}\left(A e^{j \pi B t}\right)+\operatorname{Real}\left(D e^{j \pi E t}\right)$. What are B,E?

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: The following signal $x_1(t)=\cos (3 \pi t)-4 \cos (5 \pi t-0.5 \pi)$ can be expressed as $x_1(t)=\operatorname{Real}\left(A e^{j \pi B t}\right)+\operatorname{Real}\left(D e^{j \pi E t}\right)$. What are B,E?
A. [2, 5]
B. [5, 3]
C. [7, 4]
D. [1, 6]
E. [5, 2]
F. [3, 5]
G. [2, 4]
H. [6, 1]
I. [3, 7]
J. [4, 7]
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The first term \(\cos(3\pi t)=\operatorname{Real}\{e^{j3\pi t}\}\) corresponds to \(\pi B t = 3\pi t\), so \(B=3\).

The second term \(-4\cos(5\pi t-0.5\pi)=\operatorname{Real}\{-4e^{-j0.5\pi}e^{j5\pi t}\}\) corresponds to \(\pi E t = 5\pi t\), so \(E=5\).

Thus \((B,E) = (3,5)\), which matches option **F**.

**the answer is (F)**

doc.question: You want to cluster 7 points into 3 clusters using the k-Means Clustering algorithm. Suppose after the first iteration, clusters C1, C2 and C3 contain the following two-dimensional points: C1 contains the 2 points: {(0,6), (6,0)} C2 contains the 3 points: {(2,2), (4,4), (6,6)} C3 contains the 2 points: {(5,5), (7,7)} What are the cluster centers computed for these 3 clusters?

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: You want to cluster 7 points into 3 clusters using the k-Means Clustering algorithm. Suppose after the first iteration, clusters C1, C2 and C3 contain the following two-dimensional points: C1 contains the 2 points: {(0,6), (6,0)} C2 contains the 3 points: {(2,2), (4,4), (6,6)} C3 contains the 2 points: {(5,5), (7,7)} What are the cluster centers computed for these 3 clusters?
A. C1: (3,3), C2: (6,6), C3: (12,12)
B. C1: (6,6), C2: (12,12), C3: (12,12)
C. C1: (3,3), C2: (4,4), C3: (6,6)
D. C1: (1,1), C2: (4,4), C3: (6,6)
E. C1: (0,0), C2: (4,4), C3: (6,6)
F. C1: (6,0), C2: (4,4), C3: (7,7)
G. C1: (3,3), C2: (2,2), C3: (7,7)
H. C1: (2,2), C2: (5,5), C3: (7,7)
I. C1: (0,0), C2: (48,48), C3: (35,35)
J. C1: (0,6), C2: (2,2), C3: (5,5)
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The centroids are the averages of the points in each cluster:

- **C1:** \((0,6)\) and \((6,0)\) → \(\big(\frac{0+6}{2},\frac{6+0}{2}\big) = (3,3)\)
- **C2:** \((2,2), (4,4), (6,6)\) → \(\big(\frac{2+4+6}{3},\frac{2+4+6}{3}\big) = (4,4)\)
- **C3:** \((5,5)\) and \((7,7)\) → \(\big(\frac{5+7}{2},\frac{5+7}{2}\big) = (6,6)\)

Thus the correct set of cluster centers is:

**C1: (3,3), C2: (4,4), C3: (6,6)**

The answer is (C).

A. 56, 65
B. 65, 56
C. 66, 55
D. 55, 67
E. 55, 66
F. 55, 55
G. 65, 67
H. 67, 55
I. 66, 66
J. 56, 66
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The program modifies and accesses the same variable (`x` or `y`) within a single `printf` call without a sequence point, which makes the behavior undefined in standard C. However, on typical implementations that evaluate function arguments from right to left, the first `printf` prints `5` (from `x++`) followed by `6` (the updated `x`), and the second `printf` prints `6` (from `++y`) followed by `6` (the updated `y`). This yields:

```
56
66
```

So, among the given choices, the one that matches this typical output is:

**J. 56, 66**

the answer is (J)

doc.question: We are interested in the capacity of photographic film. The film consists of silver iodide crystals, Poisson distributed, with a density of 100 particles per unit area. Suppose that the temperature of a cup of coffee obeys Newton's law of cooling. If the coffee has a temperature of $200^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$ when freshly poured, and $1 \mathrm{~min}$ later has cooled to $190^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$ in a room at $70^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$, determine when the coffee reaches a temperature of $150^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$.

If the coffee has a temperature of $200^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$ when freshly poured, and $1 \mathrm{~min}$ later has cooled to $190^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$ in a room at $70^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$, determine when the coffee reaches a temperature of $150^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$.
A. 10.2 min
B. 7.8 min
C. 12.0 min
D. 9.5 min
E. 2.8 min
F. 4.5 min
G. 5.2 min
H. 8.4 min
I. 3.3 min
J. 6.07 min

resps:
Using Newton’s law of cooling  

\[
\frac{dT}{dt}=-k\,(T-70),\qquad T(t)=70+(T_0-70)e^{-kt}.
\]

With \(T_0=200^\circ\!F\),

\[
190 = 70 + 130e^{-k}\;\Longrightarrow\;e^{-k}= \frac{120}{130}= \frac{12}{13},
\]
\[
k = -\ln\!\left(\frac{12}{13}\right)=\ln\!\left(\frac{13}{12}\right)\approx 0.0800\ \text{min}^{-1}.
\]

For \(T=150^\circ\!F\),

\[
150 = 70 + 130e^{-kt}\;\Longrightarrow\;e^{-kt}= \frac{80}{130}= \frac{8}{13},
\]
\[
-kt = \ln\!\left(\frac{8}{13}\right)\approx -0.4855,
\]
\[
t = \frac{0.4855}{k}\approx \frac{0.4855}{0.0800}\approx 6.07\text{ min}.
\]

Thus the coffee reaches \(150^\circ\!F\) after about **6.07 minutes**.

**the answer is (J)**

If every student must use a computer and there can only be 2 students on a computer at most, what is the maximum number of students who can have a computer to themselves?
A. 2
B. 18
C. 8
D. 6
E. 16
F. 12
G. 20
H. 10
I. 24
J. 14
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The class has 18 working computers.  
Let \(x\) be the number of computers used by a single student and \(y\) the number used by two students.

\[
\begin{cases}
x + 2y = 26 &\text{(all 26 students must be seated)}\\
x + y \le 18 &\text{(only 18 functional computers)}
\end{cases}
\]

From the first equation, \(y = \frac{26 - x}{2}\).  
Substituting into the second inequality:

\[
x + \frac{26 - x}{2} \le 18 \\
\frac{x + 26}{2} \le 18 \\
x + 26 \le 36 \\
x \le 10.
\]

Since \(x\) must be an integer and the expression \((26 - x)/2\) must be integral, \(x\) must be even. The largest even integer \(\le 10\) is \(10\).

Thus, at most 10 students can have a computer to themselves.

The correct choice is **H. 10**.

**the answer is (H)**

\[

doc.question: One model for the spread of an epidemic is that the rate of spread is jointly proportional to the number of infected people and the number of uninfected people. How long does it take for $80 \%$ of the population to become infected?

In an isolated town of 5000 inhabitants, 160 people have a disease at the beginning of the week and 1200 have it at the end of the week. How long does it take for $80 \%$ of the population to become infected?
A. 22 days
B. 20 $\mathrm{days}$
C. 12 days
D. 18 days
E. 8 days
F. 30 days
G. 25 $\mathrm{days}$
H. 10 $\mathrm{days}$
I. 15 $\mathrm{days}$
J. 35 days
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The logistic model gives  

\[
\frac{dI}{dt}=kI(N-I),\qquad N=5000.
\]

Its solution is  

\[
I(t)=\frac{N}{1+C e^{-kNt}},\qquad C=\frac{N}{I_0}-1.
\]

With \(I_0=160\) we have \(C=5000/160-1=30.25\).

Using \(I(7\text{ days})=1200\),

\[
1200=\frac{5000}{1+30.25e^{-k\cdot5000\cdot7}}
\Longrightarrow e^{-35000k}=0.1047,
\]
so \(k=\dfrac{-\ln(0.1047)}{35000}\approx6.44\times10^{-5}\).

For \(80\%\) infection, \(I=0.8N=4000\):

\[
4000=\frac{5000}{1+30.25e^{-k\cdot5000\,t}}
\Longrightarrow e^{-5000kt}=0.00826,
\]
\[
t=\frac{-\ln(0.00826)}{5000k}\approx\frac{4.79}{0.322}\approx14.9\text{ days}.
\]

Thus it takes about **15 days** for 80 % of the population to become infected.

**The answer is (I).**

doc.question:
Let R be a ring with a multiplicative identity. If U is an additive subgroup of R such that ur in U for all u in U and for all r in R, then U is said to be a right ideal of R. If R has exactly two right ideals, which of the following must be true?
I. R is commutative.
II. R is a division ring (that is, all elements except the additive identity have multiplicative inverses).
III. R is infinite.
choices: R is a ring without a multiplicative identity

[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: Let R be a ring with a multiplicative identity. If U is an additive subgroup of R such that ur in U for all u in U and for all r in R, then U is said to be a right ideal of R. If R has exactly two right ideals, which of the following must be true?
I. R is commutative.
II. R is a division ring (that is, all elements except the additive identity have multiplicative inverses).
III. R is infinite.
A. R is a finite ring
B. R is a ring without a multiplicative identity
C. II and III only
D. II only
E. I and III only
F. I and II only
G. None of the above
H. III only
I. I only
J. I, II and III
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The condition that \(R\) has exactly two right ideals (\(\{0\}\) and \(R\) itself) forces every non‑zero element \(a\in R\) to generate the whole right ideal \(aR=R\). Hence there exists \(r\in R\) with \(ar=1\); each non‑zero element has a right inverse, and a standard argument then shows it also has a left inverse, so every non‑zero element is invertible. Thus \(R\) is a division ring.

A division ring need not be commutative (e.g., the quaternions), and it can be either finite or infinite (finite division rings are fields). Therefore only statement II must hold.

The correct choice is **D. II only**.  

**the answer is (D)**

doc.question: suppose a,b,c,\alpha,\beta,\gamma are six real numbers with a^2+b^2+c^2>0.  In addition, $a=b*cos(\gamma)+c*cos(\beta), b=c*cos(\alpha)+a*cos(\gamma), c=a*cos(\beta)+b*cos(\alpha)$. In addition, $a=b*cos(\gamma)+c*cos(\beta), b=c*cos(\alpha)+a*cos(\gamma), c=a*cos(\beta)+b*cos(\alpha)$. What is the value of $cos^2(\alpha)+cos^2(\beta)+cos^2(\gamma)+2*cos(\alpha)*cos(\beta)*cos(\gamma)? return the numeric.
A. 0.5
B. 0.0
C. 2.0
D. 1.0
E. -0.5
F. 1.5
G. -1.0
H. 2.5
I. 3.0
J. 0.25
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The given equations can be written in matrix form  

\[
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & -\cos\gamma & -\cos\beta\\
-\cos\gamma & 1 & -\cos\alpha\\
-\cos\beta & -\cos\alpha & 1
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}a\\ b\\ c\end{pmatrix}=0 .
\]

Since \((a,b,c)\neq(0,0,0)\), the determinant of the matrix must be zero.  
Let \(x=\cos\alpha,\;y=\cos\beta,\;z=\cos\gamma\). Then

\[
\det\begin{pmatrix}
1 & -z & -y\\
-z & 1 & -x\\
-y & -x & 1
\end{pmatrix}=0 .
\]

Computing the determinant:

\[
\begin{aligned}
D &= 1(1\cdot1 - (-x)(-x)) - (-z)(-z\cdot1 - (-x)(-y)) + (-y)((-z)(-x) - 1(-y))\\
  &= (1 - x^{2}) - z^{2} - xyz - xyz - y^{2}\\
  &= 1 - (x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}) - 2xyz .
\end{aligned}
\]

Setting \(D=0\) gives  

\[
x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+2xyz = 1 .
\]

But the required expression is exactly  

\[
\cos^{2}\alpha+\cos^{2}\beta+\cos^{2}\gamma+2\cos\alpha\cos\beta\cos\gamma = x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+2xyz .
\]

Hence its value is \(1\).

The correct choice is **D. 1.0**.  

the answer is (D)

The problem is asking for the limiting probability that a random permutation of \(n\) elements has at least one fixed point (a “match”).  
For a permutation of \(n\) items, the probability of **no** matches (a derangement) is  

\[
\frac{D_n}{n!}= \sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{(-1)^k}{k!}\;,
\]

which tends to \(e^{-1}= \frac{1}{e}\) as \(n\to\infty\).  
Therefore the probability of **at least one** match is  

\[
1-\frac{1}{e}.
\]

Hence the limit of the required probability is \(1-\frac{1}{e}\).

\[
\text{the answer is (A)}
\]

Let $X$ be the number that dropped out in the first group and $Y$ the number in the second group. Assuming independence where needed, give the sum that equals the probability that $Y \geq X+2$. Let $X$ be the number that dropped out in the first group and $Y$ the number in the second group. Assuming independence where needed, give the sum that equals the probability that $Y \geq X+2$. HINT: What is the distribution of $Y-X+25$ ?
A. $0.1745$
B. $0.3751$
C. $0.5012$
D. $0.3359$

H. $0.4256$

resps:
The probability can be written as  

\[
P(Y\ge X+2)=\sum_{x=0}^{25}\binom{25}{x}\left(\tfrac12\right)^{25}
            \sum_{y=x+2}^{25}\binom{25}{y}\left(\tfrac12\right)^{25}
        =\frac{1-P(D=0)-2P(D=1)}{2},
\]

where \(D=Y-X\).  
Using the generating function  

\[
\bigl(0.25t^{-1}+0.5+0.25t\bigr)^{25}
   =0.25^{25}(t^{-1}+2+t)^{25}
   =2^{-50}t^{-25}(1+t)^{50},
\]

the coefficient of \(t^{k}\) gives  

\[
P(D=k)=2^{-50}\binom{50}{k+25}.
\]

Thus  

\[
P(D=0)=2^{-50}\binom{50}{25},\qquad
P(D=1)=2^{-50}\binom{50}{26}.
\]

Hence  

\[
P(Y\ge X+2)=\frac{1-\displaystyle 2^{-50}\binom{50}{25}
               -2\cdot 2^{-50}\binom{50}{26}}{2}
           =\frac{756\,391\,980\,332\,272}
                  {2\,251\,799\,813\,685\,248}
           \approx 0.3359.
\]

The answer that matches this value is  

**D. 0.3359**.

doc.question: The amount of paint that David needs to cover a cube is directly proportional to the surface area. If David can completely cover a cube of side length 2 feet with exactly 16 quarts of paint, how big a cube (in terms of edge length in feet) can David cover with 169 quarts of paint?

resps:
The paint needed is proportional to the surface area \(6s^{2}\).  
Let \(P = k\cdot 6s^{2}=K s^{2}\).

Given \(s=2\) ft and \(P=16\) quarts:
\[
16 = K(2)^{2}=4K \;\Longrightarrow\; K = 4.
\]

So \(P = 4s^{2}\).

Set \(P = 169\) quarts:
\[
169 = 4s^{2} \;\Longrightarrow\; s^{2} = \frac{169}{4} \;\Longrightarrow\; s = \frac{13}{2}\text{ ft}.
\]

Thus the edge length of the cube David can cover with 169 quarts of paint is \(\frac{13}{2}\) feet.

The answer is (B).

The conditional probability is  

\[
P(188<X<194\mid X<194)=\frac{P(188<X<194)}{P(X<194)}.
\]

Standardizing with \(Z=\frac{X-200}{5}\):

\[
Z_{194}=\frac{194-200}{5}=-1.2,\qquad 
Z_{188}=\frac{188-200}{5}=-2.4.
\]

Using the standard normal table:

\[
\Phi(-1.2)\approx0.1151,\qquad 
\Phi(-2.4)\approx0.0082.
\]

So  

\[
P(188<X<194)=\Phi(-1.2)-\Phi(-2.4)\approx0.1151-0.0082=0.1069,
\]
\[
P(X<194)=\Phi(-1.2)\approx0.1151.
\]

Hence  

\[
\frac{0.1069}{0.1151}\approx0.93.
\]

The correct choice is **A**.

the answer is (A)

\end{aligned}
\]

Thus \(a_0 = 30 - 11 = 19\).

Number of Species A ants on Day 5:
\[
a_5 = a_0 \cdot 2^5 = 19 \times 32 = 608.
\]

So the correct choice is **608**, which is option **A**.

doc.question: Bob rolls a fair six-sided die each morning. If Bob rolls a composite number, he eats sweetened cereal. If he rolls a prime number, he eats unsweetened cereal. If he rolls a 1, then he rolls again. In a non-leap year, what is the expected number of times Bob will roll his die?

resps:
Each morning Bob rolls until he gets a result other than 1.  
The probability of “success” (rolling 2–6) on any roll is  

\[
p = \frac{5}{6}.
\]

The number of rolls needed that morning follows a geometric distribution with expected value  

\[
E[\text{rolls per day}] = \frac{1}{p}= \frac{1}{5/6}= \frac{6}{5}=1.2.
\]

Over the 365 days of a non‑leap year, the expected total number of rolls is  

\[
365 \times \frac{6}{5}= \frac{365\cdot 6}{5}= \frac{2190}{5}=438.
\]

Thus the expected number of times Bob will roll his die is **438**, which corresponds to option **E**.

**the answer is (E)**

doc.question:
Water drips out of a hole at the vertex of an upside down cone at a rate of 3 cm^3 per minute. The
cone’s height and radius are 2 cm and 1 cm, respectively. At what rate does the height of the water change
when the water level is half a centimeter below the top of the cone? The volume of a cone is V = (π/3)*r^2*h,
where r is the radius and h is the height of the cone.

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: Water drips out of a hole at the vertex of an upside down cone at a rate of 3 cm^3 per minute. The
cone’s height and radius are 2 cm and 1 cm, respectively. At what rate does the height of the water change
when the water level is half a centimeter below the top of the cone? The volume of a cone is V = (π/3)*r^2*h,
where r is the radius and h is the height of the cone.
A. −28/(3π) cm/min
B. −8/(3π) cm/min
C. −24/(3π) cm/min
D. −48/π cm/min
E. −12/(3π) cm/min
F. −4/(3π) cm/min
G. −32/(3π) cm/min
H. −20/(3π) cm/min
I. −40/(3π) cm/min
J. −16/(3π) cm/min
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The volume of water in the cone at height \(h\) is  

\[
V=\frac{\pi}{3}r^{2}h,\qquad r=\frac{R}{H}h=\frac{1}{2}h
\]

so  

\[
V=\frac{\pi}{3}\left(\frac{h}{2}\right)^{2}h=\frac{\pi}{12}h^{3}.
\]

Differentiate with respect to time:

\[
\frac{dV}{dt}= \frac{\pi}{12}\cdot 3h^{2}\frac{dh}{dt}= \frac{\pi}{4}h^{2}\frac{dh}{dt}.
\]

The water is draining, so \(\frac{dV}{dt} = -3\ \text{cm}^3/\text{min}\). Hence  

\[
-3 = \frac{\pi}{4}h^{2}\frac{dh}{dt}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad 
\frac{dh}{dt}= -\frac{12}{\pi h^{2}}.
\]

When the water level is \(0.5\) cm below the top, the height of the water is  

\[
h = 2 - 0.5 = 1.5\ \text{cm},
\qquad h^{2}= (1.5)^{2}=2.25.
\]

Thus  

\[
\frac{dh}{dt}= -\frac{12}{\pi(2.25)} = -\frac{12}{\frac{9}{4}\pi}= -\frac{48}{9\pi}= -\frac{16}{3\pi}\ \text{cm/min}.
\]

The corresponding choice is  

**J. \(-\dfrac{16}{3\pi}\) cm/min**.  

the answer is (J)

doc.question:
If $R$ is the total resistance of three resistors, connected in parallel, with resistances $R_1, R_2, R_3$, then
$$
\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{R_1}+\frac{1}{R_2}+\frac{1}{R_3}
$$
If the resistances are measured in ohms as $R_1=25 \Omega$, $R_2=40 \Omega$, and $R_3=50 \Omega$, with a possible error of $0.5 \%$ in each case, estimate the maximum error in the calculated value of $R$.

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: If $R$ is the total resistance of three resistors, connected in parallel, with resistances $R_1, R_2, R_3$, then
$$
\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{R_1}+\frac{1}{R_2}+\frac{1}{R_3}
$$
If the resistances are measured in ohms as $R_1=25 \Omega$, $R_2=40 \Omega$, and $R_3=50 \Omega$, with a possible error of $0.5 \%$ in each case, estimate the maximum error in the calculated value of $R$.
A. $\frac{1}{20}$ $\Omega$
B. $\frac{1}{10}$ $\Omega$
C. $\frac{1}{14}$ $\Omega$
D. $\frac{1}{25}$ $\Omega$
E. $\frac{1}{15}$ $\Omega$
F. $\frac{1}{12}$ $\Omega$
G. $\frac{1}{22}$ $\Omega$
H. $\frac{1}{17}$ $\Omega$
I. $\frac{1}{30}$ $\Omega$
J. $\frac{1}{18}$ $\Omega$
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The total conductance is  

\[
\frac1R = \frac1{25}+\frac1{40}+\frac1{50}=0.04+0.025+0.02=0.085\;\text{S},
\qquad 
R=\frac1{0.085}\approx 11.76\;\Omega .
\]

Let \(S=1/R\).  For small changes  

\[
dR = -\frac{1}{S^{2}}\,dS = -R^{2}\,dS .
\]

Since \(S=\sum 1/R_i\),

\[
dS = \sum \left(-\frac{1}{R_i^{2}}\,dR_i\right),\qquad 
|dS|\le \sum \frac{|dR_i|}{R_i^{2}} .
\]

Each resistance has a possible relative error of \(0.5\% =0.005\), so  

\[
|dR_i| = 0.005\,R_i \;\Longrightarrow\; 
\frac{|dR_i|}{R_i^{2}} = \frac{0.005}{R_i}.
\]

Hence  

\[
|dS|\le 0.005\left(\frac1{25}+\frac1{40}+\frac1{50}\right)
      =0.005(0.085)=0.000425 .
\]

Now  

\[
|dR|_{\max}=R^{2}\,|dS|
            \approx (11.76)^{2}\times 0.000425
            \approx 0.059\;\Omega .
\]

The value \(0.059\;\Omega\) corresponds to \(\frac1{17}\;\Omega\).

\[
\boxed{\text{H}}
\]

doc.question: A publisher used standard boxes for shipping books. The mean weight of books packed per box is 25 pounds, with a standard deviation of two pounds. The mean weight of the boxes is one pound, with a standard deviation of 0.15 pounds. The mean weight of the packing material used per box is two pounds, with a standard deviation of 0.25 pounds. What is the standard deviation of the weights of the packed boxes?

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: A publisher used standard boxes for shipping books. The mean weight of books packed per box is 25 pounds, with a standard deviation of two pounds. The mean weight of the boxes is one pound, with a standard deviation of 0.15 pounds. The mean weight of the packing material used per box is two pounds, with a standard deviation of 0.25 pounds. What is the standard deviation of the weights of the packed boxes?
A. 28.000 pounds
B. 1.950 pounds
C. 2.500 pounds
D. 4.085 pounds
E. 3.012 pounds
F. 3.500 pounds
G. 5.290 pounds
H. 2.021 pounds
I. 2.250 pounds
J. 1.785 pounds
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The total weight of a packed box is the sum of three independent components: the books, the box, and the packing material.  
For independent variables, the variance of the sum equals the sum of the variances.

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var(books)} &= (2)^2 = 4.00,\\
\text{Var(box)} &= (0.15)^2 = 0.0225,\\
\text{Var(packing)} &= (0.25)^2 = 0.0625.
\end{aligned}
\]

Add the variances:

\[
\text{Var(total)} = 4.00 + 0.0225 + 0.0625 = 4.085.
\]

The standard deviation is the square root of the variance:

\[
\text{SD(total)} = \sqrt{4.085} \approx 2.021 \text{ pounds}.
\]

Thus the correct choice is **2.021 pounds**.

**the answer is (H)**

The diffusion flux of O₂ is obtained from Fick’s law  

\[
N_{O_2}= -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT}\,\frac{\Delta P}{\Delta z}
\]

with  

\[
\Delta P = 13500-6000 = 7500\;{\rm Pa},\qquad 
\Delta z = 2.2\times10^{-3}\;{\rm m},\qquad 
T = 273.15\;{\rm K},\qquad 
R = 8.314\;{\rm J\,mol^{-1}K^{-1}}
\]

\[
\frac{D_{O_2-N_2}}{RT}= \frac{1.81\times10^{-5}}{8.314\times273.15}=7.97\times10^{-9}\;{\rm m^{2}\,Pa^{-1}\,s^{-1}}
\]

\[
\frac{\Delta P}{\Delta z}= \frac{7500}{2.2\times10^{-3}}=3.41\times10^{6}\;{\rm Pa\,m^{-1}}
\]

\[
N_{O_2}=7.97\times10^{-9}\times3.41\times10^{6}=2.72\times10^{-2}\;{\rm mol\,m^{-2}\,s^{-1}}
      =2.72\times10^{-5}\;{\rm kmol\,m^{-2}\,s^{-1}}
\]

---

For a mixture of N₂ and CO₂ (volume ratio 2:1, i.e. \(y_{N_2}=2/3,\;y_{CO_2}=1/3\)) the effective diffusivity is

\[
\frac{1}{D_{\text{eff}}}= \frac{y_{N_2}}{D_{O_2-N_2}}+\frac{y_{CO_2}}{D_{O_2-CO_2}}
   =\frac{0.6667}{1.81\times10^{-5}}+\frac{0.3333}{1.85\times10^{-5}}
   =5.48\times10^{4}
\]

\[
D_{\text{eff}} = 1.82\times10^{-5}\;{\rm m^{2}\,s^{-1}}
\]

Using this \(D_{\text{eff}}\) in Fick’s law gives

\[
N_{O_2}= \frac{1.82\times10^{-5}}{8.314\times273.15}\,
          \frac{7500}{2.2\times10^{-3}}
        =2.74\times10^{-5}\;{\rm kmol\,m^{-2}\,s^{-1}}
\]

Thus  

- (I) \(N_{O_2}=2.72\times10^{-5}\;{\rm kmol\,m^{-2}\,s^{-1}}\)  
- (II) \(N_{O_2}=2.74\times10^{-5}\;{\rm kmol\,m^{-2}\,s^{-1}}\)

The pair of values that best matches these results is:

**the answer is (H)**.

doc.question: Assume that all gases are perfect and that data refer to 298.15 K unless otherwise stated. Consider a system consisting of $2.0 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$, initially at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $10 \mathrm{~atm}$ and confined to a cylinder of cross-section $10.0 \mathrm{~cm}^2$. It is allowed to expand adiabatically against an external pressure of 1.0 atm until the piston has moved outwards through $20 \mathrm{~cm}$. Assume that carbon dioxide may be considered a perfect gas with $C_{V, \mathrm{~m}}=28.8 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and calculate $\Delta U$.

Consider a system consisting of $2.0 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$, initially at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $10 \mathrm{~atm}$ and confined to a cylinder of cross-section $10.0 \mathrm{~cm}^2$. It is allowed to expand adiabatically against an external pressure of 1.0 atm until the piston has moved outwards through $20 \mathrm{~cm}$. Assume that carbon dioxide may be considered a perfect gas with $C_{V, \mathrm{~m}}=28.8 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and calculate $\Delta U$.
A. -30$\text{J}$

F. -20$\text{J}$
G. 30 J
H. -70 J
I. -50 J
J. -10$\text{J}$
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The expansion is adiabatic, so \(q=0\).  
The change in internal energy is therefore equal to the work done on the system:

\[
\Delta U = w = -P_{\text{ext}}\Delta V
\]

The piston moves \(0.20\ \text{m}\) and the cross‑sectional area is  

\[
A = 10.0\ \text{cm}^2 = 10.0\times10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2 = 1.0\times10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2
\]

Hence  

\[
\Delta V = A\Delta x = (1.0\times10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2)(0.20\ \text{m}) = 2.0\times10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3
\]

Using \(P_{\text{ext}} = 1.0\ \text{atm}=1.01325\times10^{5}\ \text{Pa}\),

\[
\Delta U = - (1.01325\times10^{5}\ \text{Pa})(2.0\times10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3)
          = -20.3\ \text{J}\approx -20\ \text{J}
\]

Thus \(\Delta U \approx -20\ \text{J}\).

**The answer is (F).**

doc.question: The van der Waal equation is a modification of the ideal gas equation. It reads [P + (an^2 / V^2)] (V - nb) = nRT where P = pressure, V = volume, n = number of moles, R = gas constant, T = absolute temperature, and (a) and (b) are constants for a particular gas. The term an^2 /V^2 corrects the pressure for intermolecular attraction and the term - nb corrects the volume for molecular volume. Using this equation, determine whether a gas becomes more or less ideal when: (a.) the gas is compressed at constant temperature; (b.) more gas is added at constant volume and temperature; and (c.) The temperature of the gas is raised at constant volume.

less ideal, more ideal, less ideal
more ideal, less ideal, less ideal
closer to being ideal, more ideal, less ideal
less ideal, more ideal, more ideal
less ideal, less ideal, less ideal
more ideal, more ideal, less ideal
less ideal, less ideal, closer to being ideal
more ideal, less ideal, closer to being ideal
closer to being ideal, less ideal, more ideal

doc.question: One of the methods for the synthesis of dilute acetic acid from ethanol is the use ofacetobacter. A dilute solution of ethanol is allowed to trickle down overbeechwoodshavings that have been inoculated with a culture of the bacteria. Air is forced through the vat countercurrent to the alcohol flow. The reaction can be written as C_2H_5OH (aq) + O_2 CH_3COOH (aq) + H_2O (l) acetic acid Calculate the Gibbs Free Energy, ∆G°, for this reaction, given the following Gibbs Free Energies of Formation: Ethanol = - 43.39 Kcal/mole, H_2O (l) = - 56.69 Kcal/mole, and Acetic acid = - 95.38 Kcal/mole.

A. - 89.1 Kcal/mole

resps:
The standard Gibbs free energy change is  

\[
\Delta G^\circ = \sum \Delta G_f^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta G_f^\circ(\text{reactants})
\]

\[
\Delta G^\circ = [\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}) + \Delta G_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O})] - [\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}) + \Delta G_f^\circ(\text{O}_2)]
\]

\[
\Delta G^\circ = [(-95.38) + (-56.69)] - [(-43.39) + 0] = -152.07 + 43.39 = -108.68\ \text{kcal/mol}
\]

Rounded to the given choices, this corresponds to \(-108.7\) kcal/mol.

**The answer is (G).**

doc.question: Nelson, et al. (Science 238, 1670 (1987)) examined several weakly bound gas-phase complexes of ammonia in search of examples in which the $\mathrm{H}$ atoms in $\mathrm{NH}_3$ formed hydrogen bonds, but found none. For example, they found that the complex of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ and $\mathrm{CO}_2$ has the carbon atom nearest the nitrogen (299 pm away): the $\mathrm{CO}_2$ molecule is at right angles to the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{N}$ 'bond', and the $\mathrm{H}$ atoms of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ are pointing away from the $\mathrm{CO}_2$. The magnitude of the permanent dipole moment of this complex is reported as $1.77 \mathrm{D}$. If the $\mathrm{N}$ and $\mathrm{C}$ atoms are the centres of the negative and positive charge distributions, respectively, what is the magnitude of those partial charges (as multiples of $e$ )?

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: Nelson, et al. (Science 238, 1670 (1987)) examined several weakly bound gas-phase complexes of ammonia in search of examples in which the $\mathrm{H}$ atoms in $\mathrm{NH}_3$ formed hydrogen bonds, but found none. For example, they found that the complex of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ and $\mathrm{CO}_2$ has the carbon atom nearest the nitrogen (299 pm away): the $\mathrm{CO}_2$ molecule is at right angles to the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{N}$ 'bond', and the $\mathrm{H}$ atoms of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ are pointing away from the $\mathrm{CO}_2$. The magnitude of the permanent dipole moment of this complex is reported as $1.77 \mathrm{D}$. If the $\mathrm{N}$ and $\mathrm{C}$ atoms are the centres of the negative and positive charge distributions, respectively, what is the magnitude of those partial charges (as multiples of $e$ )?
A. 0.150
B. 0.123
C. 0.135
D. 0.098
E. 0.157
F. 0.091
G. 0.112
H. 0.176
I. 0.204
J. 0.085
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The dipole moment μ is related to the charge separation q and the distance d by  

\[
\mu = q\,d \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad q = \frac{\mu}{d}.
\]

Convert the given values to SI units:

* \( \mu = 1.77\ \text{D}=1.77 \times 3.33564\times10^{-30}\ \text{C·m}=5.90\times10^{-30}\ \text{C·m}\).
* \( d = 299\ \text{pm}=299\times10^{-12}\ \text{m}=2.99\times10^{-10}\ \text{m}\).

Now  

\[
q = \frac{5.90\times10^{-30}\ \text{C·m}}{2.99\times10^{-10}\ \text{m}}
   = 1.97\times10^{-20}\ \text{C}.
\]

Express this as a fraction of the elementary charge \(e = 1.602\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}\):

\[
\frac{q}{e}= \frac{1.97\times10^{-20}}{1.602\times10^{-19}} \approx 0.123.
\]

Thus the magnitude of each partial charge is about \(0.123\,e\).

**The answer is (B).**

doc.question: The discovery of the element argon by Lord Rayleigh and Sir William Ramsay had its origins in Rayleigh's measurements of the density of nitrogen with an eye toward accurate determination of its molar mass. Rayleigh prepared some samples of nitrogen by chemical reaction of nitrogencontaining compounds; under his standard conditions, a glass globe filled with this 'chemical nitrogen' had a mass of $2.2990 \mathrm{~g}$. He prepared other samples by removing oxygen, carbon dioxide, and water vapour from atmospheric air; under the same conditions, this 'atmospheric nitrogen' had a mass of $2.3102 \mathrm{~g}$ (Lord Rayleigh, Royal Institution Proceedings 14, 524 (1895)). With the hindsight of knowing accurate values for the molar masses of nitrogen and argon, compute the mole fraction of argon in the latter sample on the assumption that the former was pure nitrogen and the latter a mixture of nitrogen and argon.

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: The discovery of the element argon by Lord Rayleigh and Sir William Ramsay had its origins in Rayleigh's measurements of the density of nitrogen with an eye toward accurate determination of its molar mass. Rayleigh prepared some samples of nitrogen by chemical reaction of nitrogencontaining compounds; under his standard conditions, a glass globe filled with this 'chemical nitrogen' had a mass of $2.2990 \mathrm{~g}$. He prepared other samples by removing oxygen, carbon dioxide, and water vapour from atmospheric air; under the same conditions, this 'atmospheric nitrogen' had a mass of $2.3102 \mathrm{~g}$ (Lord Rayleigh, Royal Institution Proceedings 14, 524 (1895)). With the hindsight of knowing accurate values for the molar masses of nitrogen and argon, compute the mole fraction of argon in the latter sample on the assumption that the former was pure nitrogen and the latter a mixture of nitrogen and argon.
A. 0.022
B. 0.011
C. 0.003
D. 0.030
E. 0.008
F. 0.025
G. 0.040
H. 0.015
I. 0.018
J. 0.005
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The mass difference between the two samples (2.3102 g – 2.2990 g = 0.0112 g) is due to the presence of argon.  
Assuming the same volume, temperature and pressure, the total number of moles in each globe is the same:

\[
n_{\text{total}} = \frac{2.2990\ \text{g}}{M_{\text{N}_2}} 
= \frac{2.2990}{28.0134}\ \text{mol} \approx 0.0821\ \text{mol}
\]

The average molar mass of the atmospheric sample is therefore

\[
\bar M = \frac{2.3102\ \text{g}}{0.0821\ \text{mol}} \approx 28.16\ \text{g mol}^{-1}
\]

Let \(x\) be the mole fraction of argon. Then

\[
\bar M = (1-x)M_{\text{N}_2}+xM_{\text{Ar}}
= 28.0134 + x(39.948-28.0134)
\]

\[
x = \frac{28.16-28.0134}{39.948-28.0134}
\approx \frac{0.1466}{11.9346}
\approx 0.012 \; (\text{about }1.2\%)
\]

The value closest to this is \(x \approx 0.011\).

**Answer: the answer is (B)**

doc.question: An automobile with a mass of $1000 \mathrm{~kg}$, including passengers, settles $1.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ closer to the road for every additional $100 \mathrm{~kg}$ of passengers. It is driven with a constant horizontal component of speed $20 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ over a washboard road with sinusoidal bumps. The amplitude and wavelength of the sine curve are $5.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $20 \mathrm{~cm}$, respectively. The distance between the front and back wheels is $2.4 \mathrm{~m}$. Find the amplitude of oscillation of the automobile, assuming it moves vertically as an undamped driven harmonic oscillator. Neglect the mass of the wheels and springs and assume that the wheels are always in contact with the road.

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: An automobile with a mass of $1000 \mathrm{~kg}$, including passengers, settles $1.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ closer to the road for every additional $100 \mathrm{~kg}$ of passengers. It is driven with a constant horizontal component of speed $20 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ over a washboard road with sinusoidal bumps. The amplitude and wavelength of the sine curve are $5.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $20 \mathrm{~cm}$, respectively. The distance between the front and back wheels is $2.4 \mathrm{~m}$. Find the amplitude of oscillation of the automobile, assuming it moves vertically as an undamped driven harmonic oscillator. Neglect the mass of the wheels and springs and assume that the wheels are always in contact with the road.
A. -0.1 $ \mathrm{~mm}$
B. -0.3 $\mathrm{~mm}$
C. 0.05 $ \mathrm{~mm}$
D. 0.25 $\mathrm{~mm}$
E. -0.16 $ \mathrm{~mm}$
F. -0.25 $\mathrm{~mm}$
G. 0.2 $ \mathrm{~mm}$
H. 1.0 $\mathrm{~mm}$
I. 0.1 $\mathrm{~mm}$
J. -0.5 $\mathrm{~mm}$
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The static deflection change gives the effective spring constant  

\[
k=\frac{\Delta (mg)}{\Delta \delta}= \frac{100\,\text{kg}\,g}{0.01\ \text{m}}
      =\frac{100(9.8)}{0.01}=9.8\times10^{4}\ \text{N/m}.
\]

The natural angular frequency of the car‑body (treated as a single mass‑spring system) is  

\[
\omega_0=\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}=\sqrt{\frac{9.8\times10^{4}}{1000}}
        =\sqrt{98}\approx 9.9\ \text{rad s}^{-1}.
\]

The road profile is  

\[
y_r(x)=A\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi x}{\lambda}\right),\qquad 
A=0.05\ \text{m},\ \lambda=0.20\ \text{m}.
\]

With the car moving at \(v=20\ \text{km h}^{-1}=5.56\ \text{m s}^{-1}\),

\[
\omega_d = \frac{2\pi v}{\lambda}= \frac{2\pi(5.56)}{0.20}
          \approx 1.75\times10^{2}\ \text{rad s}^{-1}.
\]

Because the wheelbase \(L=2.4\ \text{m}=12\lambda\), the front and rear wheels see the same road phase, so the two springs act together as a single spring of constant \(k\).

For an undamped driven oscillator  

\[
m\ddot y + k y = k y_r(t),
\]

the steady‑state amplitude is  

\[
Y = \frac{k}{m}\,\frac{A}{\lvert\omega_0^{2}-\omega_d^{2}\rvert}
    =\frac{\omega_0^{2}A}{\lvert\omega_0^{2}-\omega_d^{2}\rvert}.
\]

Insert the numbers:

\[
\omega_0^{2}=98\ \text{s}^{-2},\qquad 
\omega_d^{2}\approx (174.5)^{2}=3.05\times10^{4}\ \text{s}^{-2},
\]

\[
Y = \frac{98\,(0.05)}{3.05\times10^{4}-98}
    \approx 1.6\times10^{-4}\ \text{m}=0.16\ \text{mm}.
\]

Since \(\omega_d \gg \omega_0\), the response is \(180^{\circ}\) out of phase with the road, giving a negative sign for the displacement relative to the road profile.

\[
\boxed{-0.16\ \text{mm}}
\]

Thus the correct choice is **E**.

doc.question: A steel cylinder contains liquid at a mean bulk temperature of 80°F. Steam condensing at 212°F on the outside surface is used for heating the liquid. The coefficient of heat transfer on the steam side is 1,000 Btu/hr-ft^2-°F. The liquid is agitated by the stirring action of a turbine impeller. Its diameter is 2 ft., and it moves at an angular velocity of 100 rpm. The cylinder is 6 ft. long, with a diameter of 6 ft. and a wall thickness of 1/8 in. The thermal conductivity of steel may be taken as 9.4 Btu/hr-ft^2-°F. Properties of the liquid, taken as constant, are: c_p = 0.6 Btu/lbm-°Fk = 0.1 Btu/hr-ft-°F \rho = 60lbm/ft^3 The viscosity at 130°F is 653.4lbm/ft-hr, and at 212°F is 113.74lbm/ft-hr. Calculate the time required to raise the mean bulk temperature of the liquid to 180°F.

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: A steel cylinder contains liquid at a mean bulk temperature of 80°F. Steam condensing at 212°F on the outside surface is used for heating the liquid. The coefficient of heat transfer on the steam side is 1,000 Btu/hr-ft^2-°F. The liquid is agitated by the stirring action of a turbine impeller. Its diameter is 2 ft., and it moves at an angular velocity of 100 rpm. The cylinder is 6 ft. long, with a diameter of 6 ft. and a wall thickness of 1/8 in. The thermal conductivity of steel may be taken as 9.4 Btu/hr-ft^2-°F. Properties of the liquid, taken as constant, are: c_p = 0.6 Btu/lbm-°Fk = 0.1 Btu/hr-ft-°F \rho = 60lbm/ft^3 The viscosity at 130°F is 653.4lbm/ft-hr, and at 212°F is 113.74lbm/ft-hr. Calculate the time required to raise the mean bulk temperature of the liquid to 180°F.
A. 3.5 hr
B. 1.92 hr
C. 4.0 hr
D. 1.75 hr
E. 2.0 hr
F. 3.0 hr
G. 2.5 hr
H. 1.5 hr
I. 2.75 hr
J. 2.15 hr
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
First find the overall heat‑transfer coefficient.

**Inside convection (agitated liquid)**  

Tip speed of the impeller  

\[
\omega =100\;\text{rev/min}= \frac{100(2\pi)}{60}=10.47\;\text{rad/s}
\]

\[
v_{\text{tip}} =\omega\frac{D}{2}=10.47\;(1\;\text{ft})=10.47\;\text{ft/s}=3.77\times10^{4}\;\text{ft/hr}
\]

Reynolds number (using the viscosity at the mean temperature ≈130 °F)

\[
\text{Re}= \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}= \frac{60(3.77\times10^{4})(2)}{653.4}\approx 6.9\times10^{3}
\]

Prandtl number  

\[
\text{Pr}= \frac{\mu c_p}{k}= \frac{653.4(0.6)}{0.1}\approx 3.9\times10^{3}
\]

For a stirred tank (Kreith correlation)

\[
\text{Nu}=0.36\,\text{Re}^{0.55}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}
          \approx0.36(6.9\times10^{3})^{0.55}(3.9\times10^{3})^{1/3}
          \approx 7.4\times10^{2}
\]

\[
h_i = \frac{\text{Nu}\,k}{D}= \frac{7.36\times10^{2}(0.1)}{2}\approx 3.7\times10^{1}
      \;\text{Btu/hr·ft}^2\!·^\circ\!F
\]

**Outside convection**

\[
h_o = 1.0\times10^{3}\;\text{Btu/hr·ft}^2\!·^\circ\!F
\]

**Conduction through the steel wall**

\[
\frac{t}{k}= \frac{1/8\;\text{in}=0.0104\;\text{ft}}{9.4}=1.11\times10^{-3}
\;\text{ft}^2\!·\text{hr}/\text{Btu}
\]

Overall resistance per unit area  

\[
\frac{1}{U}= \frac{1}{h_o}+ \frac{t}{k}+ \frac{1}{h_i}
            =0.001+0.00111+0.0272=0.0293
\]

\[
U = 34\;\text{Btu/hr·ft}^2\!·^\circ\!F
\]

**Heat‑transfer area (cylinder + ends)**  

\[
A = \pi D L + 2\pi\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2
   = \pi(6)(6)+2\pi(3)^2
   = 113.1+56.5 = 169.6\;\text{ft}^2
\]

\[
UA = 34(169.6)=5.79\times10^{3}\;\text{Btu/hr·}^\circ\!F
\]

**Thermal mass of the liquid**

\[
V = \pi\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 L = \pi(3)^2(6)=54\pi\approx 1.70\times10^{2}\;\text{ft}^3
\]

\[
m = \rho V = 60(1.70\times10^{2})\approx 1.02\times10^{4}\;\text{lbm}
\]

\[
mc_p = m c_p = (1.02\times10^{4})(0.6)=6.11\times10^{3}\;\text{Btu/}^\circ\!F
\]

**Transient heating (lumped‑capacitance)**  

\[
\frac{dT}{dt}= \frac{UA}{mc_p}\,(T_s-T)
\]

Integrating from \(T_0=80^\circ\!F\) to \(T=180^\circ\!F\) with steam temperature \(T_s=212^\circ\!F\):

\[
t = \frac{mc_p}{UA}\,
    \ln\!\left[\frac{T_s-T_0}{T_s-T}\right]
   = \frac{6.11\times10^{3}}{5.79\times10^{3}}
     \ln\!\left(\frac{212-80}{212-180}\right)
   = 0.1056 \times 14.2 \approx 1.5\;\text{hr}
\]

Thus the liquid reaches 180 °F in about **1.5 hours**.

**Answer: (H) 1.5 hr**

\[
\Delta L_{\text{br}} = \alpha_{\text{br}}L_{\text{br}}\Delta T
      = 1.8\times10^{-5}\times25\times40 = 0.018\ \text{cm}

Total free expansion = \(0.018+0.024 = 0.042\) cm.  
Because the ends are fixed, the rods develop a common axial stress \(\sigma\) (same force and same cross‑sectional area).  

Compatibility gives  

\[
\sigma\Bigl(\frac{L_{\text{br}}}{E_{\text{br}}}+\frac{L_{\text{st}}}{E_{\text{st}}}\Bigr)
      = (\alpha_{\text{br}}L_{\text{br}}+\alpha_{\text{st}}L_{\text{st}})\Delta T .
\]

\[
\sigma = \frac{0.042}{\displaystyle\frac{25}{2.0\times10^{12}}+\frac{50}{1.0\times10^{12}}}
        = \frac{0.042}{1.25\times10^{-11}+5.0\times10^{-11}}
        = \frac{0.042}{6.25\times10^{-11}}
        \approx 6.7\times10^{8}\ \text{dyn cm}^{-2}
        = 0.67\times10^{9}\ \text{dyn cm}^{-2}.
\]

which corresponds to choice **H**.

          = -0.352\ \text{K}
\]

doc.question: A 25-year-old man comes to the emergency department because he developed chest pain and shortness of breath 1 hour ago, shortly after snorting cocaine for the first time. He rates the chest pain as a 7 on a 10-point scale and notes that the pain is radiating down his left arm. Medical history is unremarkable and the patient takes no medications or any other illicit drugs. He is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 70 kg (154 lb); BMI is 22 kg/m2 . The patient is diaphoretic. Vital signs are temperature 37.5°C (99.5°F), pulse 110/min, respirations 16/min, and blood pressure 200/100 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 95%. Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light and accommodation. Lungs are clear to auscultation and percussion. Auscultation of the heart discloses an audible S1 and S2. There is no edema, cyanosis, or clubbing of the digits. The patient is fully oriented. He is treated with supplemental oxygen, a 325-mg aspirin tablet, and intravenous nitroglycerin and lorazepam. Despite therapy, he continues to have chest pain and shortness of breath. ECG shows sinus tachycardia with no ST-segment or T-wave abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate additional pharmacotherapy to initiate at this time?

The patient’s ongoing chest pain despite nitrates and benzodiazepines is most consistent with cocaine‑induced coronary vasospasm. C. 2.5 × 10^3 kg of water
D. 4.0 × 10^3 kg of water
E. 1.8 × 10^3 kg of water
F. 2.2 × 10^3 kg of water
G. 3.5 × 10^3 kg of water
H. 2.0 × 10^3 kg of water
I. 1.0 × 10^3 kg of water
J. 2.8 × 10^3 kg of water
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The 2200 kg of Aerozine 50 contains equal masses of hydrazine and UDMH (1100 kg each).

**Hydrazine**
\[
\text{moles N}_2\text{H}_4 = \frac{1.10\times10^{6}\,\text{g}}{32.0\ \text{g mol}^{-1}} = 3.44\times10^{4}\ \text{mol}
\]
From the reaction \(2\text{N}_2\text{H}_4 \rightarrow 4\text{H}_2\text{O}\), each mole of N₂H₄ gives 2 mol H₂O:
\[
\text{H}_2\text{O moles} = 2(3.44\times10^{4}) = 6.88\times10^{4}\ \text{mol}
\]

**UDMH**
\[
\text{moles (CH}_3)_2\text{N}_2\text{H}_2 = \frac{1.10\times10^{6}\,\text{g}}{60.0\ \text{g mol}^{-1}} = 1.83\times10^{4}\ \text{mol}
\]
From the reaction \((\text{CH}_3)_2\text{N}_2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{H}_2\text{O}\), each mole gives 4 mol H₂O:
\[
\text{H}_2\text{O moles} = 4(1.83\times10^{4}) = 7.33\times10^{4}\ \text{mol}
\]

**Total water**
\[
\text{total moles H}_2\text{O}=6.88\times10^{4}+7.33\times10^{4}=1.42\times10^{5}\ \text{mol}
\]
\[
\text{mass H}_2\text{O}=1.42\times10^{5}\ \text{mol}\times18.0\ \text{g mol}^{-1}=2.56\times10^{6}\ \text{g}=2.6\times10^{3}\ \text{kg}
\]

Rounded to the nearest option, this is \(2.5\times10^{3}\) kg of water.

**Answer: the answer is (C).**

On arrival, he is diaphoretic. He is not oriented to person, place, or time. His temperature is 39.5°C (103.1°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 40/min, and blood pressure is 90/65 mm Hg. resps:
The presentation is classic for Lambert‑Eaton myasthenic syndrome, a paraneoplastic disorder of small‑cell lung carcinoma that causes proximal muscle weakness, diminished or absent reflexes, autonomic symptoms (dry eyes/mouth), and mild ptosis. LEMS results from autoantibodies that impair voltage‑gated calcium channels on the **presynaptic neuromuscular junction**, reducing acetylcholine release.

**Correct choice: A. Presynaptic neuromuscular junction**

doc.question: Find the sublimation rate of a uranium hexafluoride UF_6 cylinder7 mm. diameter when exposed to an air stream flowingat a velocity of 3.5 m/s. The bulk air is at 65°C and 1 atm. pressure.The surface temperature of the solidis 40°C at which its vapor pressure is 410 mm. Hg (54.65kN/m^2). The average heat transfer coefficient of fluid flowing perpendicularto a circular cylinder for fluid Reynolds number between1 and 4000 is given by Nu = 0.43 + 0.532 (Re)^0.5(Pr)^0.31 whereNu and Re are calculated with the cylinder diameter andfluid properties at mean temperature of cylinder and bulk-fluid.

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: Find the sublimation rate of a uranium hexafluoride UF_6 cylinder7 mm. diameter when exposed to an air stream flowingat a velocity of 3.5 m/s. The bulk air is at 65°C and 1 atm. pressure.The surface temperature of the solidis 40°C at which its vapor pressure is 410 mm. Hg (54.65kN/m^2). The average heat transfer coefficient of fluid flowing perpendicularto a circular cylinder for fluid Reynolds number between1 and 4000 is given by Nu = 0.43 + 0.532 (Re)^0.5(Pr)^0.31 whereNu and Re are calculated with the cylinder diameter andfluid properties at mean temperature of cylinder and bulk-fluid.
A. 29.78 kmolUF_6/m^2.s
B. 0.731 kmolUF_6/m^2.s
C. 1.442 × 10-3kmol/m2.sec. kmol/m
D. 5.23 × 10^-4 kmolUF_6/m^2.s
E. 0.850 × 10^-3 kmolUF_6/m^2.s
F. 2.56 × 10^-3 kmolUF_6/m^2.s
G. 0.415 × 10^-2 kmolUF_6/m^2.s
H. 3.67 × 10^-3 kmolUF_6/m^2.s
I. 1.789 × 10^-3 kmolUF_6/m^2.s
J. 1.12 × 10^-3kmolUF_6/m^2.s
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
First compute the Reynolds number for the air flow over the cylinder  

\[
\begin{aligned}
D &= 7\;\text{mm}=0.007\;\text{m}\\
V &= 3.5\;\text{m s}^{-1}\\[2mm]
T_{\text{mean}} &=\frac{65+40}{2}=52.5^{\circ}\text{C}=325.6\;\text{K}\\
\rho &=\frac{P}{R_{\text{air}}T}= \frac{101325}{287\; (325.6)}\approx 1.08\;\text{kg m}^{-3}\\
\mu &\approx 1.9\times10^{-5}\;\text{Pa·s}\\[2mm]
\text{Re} &=\frac{\rho V D}{\mu}= \frac{1.08(3.5)(0.007)}{1.9\times10^{-5}}\approx 1.4\times10^{3}
\end{aligned}
\]

Prandtl number for air at this temperature is \(Pr\approx0.71\).

The Nusselt correlation for \(1<Re<4000\) is  

\[
Nu =0.43+0.532\,Re^{0.5}Pr^{0.31}
\]

\[
\begin{aligned}
Re^{0.5}&=\sqrt{1400}\approx 37.4\\
Pr^{0.31}&\approx 0.71^{0.31}\approx0.90\\
Nu &\approx 0.43+0.532(37.4)(0.90)\approx 18.3
\end{aligned}
\]

Thermal conductivity of air at 325 K: \(k\approx0.028\;\text{W m}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}\).

\[
h = \frac{Nu\,k}{D}= \frac{18.3(0.028)}{0.007}\approx 73\;\text{W m}^{-2}\text{K}^{-1}
\]

The heat‑transfer analogy gives the same form for the Sherwood number, so  

\[
Sh = Nu = 18.3
\]

The binary diffusion coefficient of UF\(_6\) in air at the mean temperature is about  

\[
D_{UF_6,air}\approx 1.0\times10^{-5}\;\text{m}^2\text{s}^{-1}
\]

Hence the mass‑transfer coefficient  

\[
k_c = \frac{Sh\,D_{AB}}{D}= \frac{18.3(1.0\times10^{-5})}{0.007}\approx 2.6\times10^{-2}\;\text{m s}^{-1}
\]

Vapor pressure of UF\(_6\) at the surface temperature (40 °C) is  

\[
p_s = 410\;\text{mmHg}=0.539\;\text{atm}=5.45\times10^{4}\;\text{Pa}
\]

Molar concentration at the surface  

\[
C_s = \frac{p_s}{RT_s}= \frac{5.45\times10^{4}}{8.314\,(313)}\approx 21\;\text{mol m}^{-3}
\]

Assuming negligible UF\(_6\) in the bulk air, the molar flux is  

\[
\dot N = k_c\,C_s = (2.6\times10^{-2})(21)\approx 0.55\;\text{mol m}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}
\]

\[
\dot N = 5.5\times10^{-4}\;\text{kmol m}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}
\]

The value closest to this result among the given choices is  

\[
\boxed{5.23 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kmol UF}_6\!/\text{m}^2\!\cdot\text{s}}
\]

Thus the correct answer is **D**. the answer is (D)

doc.question: For a steady, turbulent, constant property, two dimensional boundarylayer-type flow over a flat plate at zero angle of approach, the velocity profile is given by u = v(y/\delta)^1^/7(1) where v = free stream velocity \delta = boundary layer thickness andthe local skin friction coefficient is given by C_f= [\tau / {(1/2)\rhoV^2}] = 0.045(ѵ /\rhoV)^1/4(2) where \tau = local shear stress \rho = density of the flowing fluid ѵ = kinematic viscosity Determine the local boundary layer thickness \delta, as a functionof x, by substituting the given equations into the integralform of the momentum equation.

resps:
The integral momentum equation gives  

\[
\frac{d\theta}{dx}= \frac{\tau_w}{\rho V^{2}} =\frac{C_f}{2}
\]

With the 1/7‑power‑law profile  

\[
\theta =\frac{7}{72}\,\delta ,\qquad 
C_f =0.045\left(\frac{\nu}{V\delta}\right)^{1/4}
\]

so  

\[
\frac{7}{72}\frac{d\delta}{dx}=0.0225\left(\frac{\nu}{V\delta}\right)^{1/4}
\]

which integrates to  

\[
\delta =0.37\,\frac{x}{\bigl(Re_x\bigr)^{1/5}}
\]

Hence the correct expression is  

\[
\boxed{\delta = \frac{0.37\,x}{Re_x^{1/5}}}
\]

the answer is (G).

doc.question: In an orienteering class, you have the goal of moving as far (straight-line distance) from base camp as possible by making three straight-line moves. You may use the following displacements in any order: (a) $\vec{a}, 2.0 \mathrm{~km}$ due east (directly toward the east); (b) $\vec{b}, 2.0 \mathrm{~km} 30^{\circ}$ north of east (at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ toward the north from due east); (c) $\vec{c}, 1.0 \mathrm{~km}$ due west. Alternatively, you may substitute either $-\vec{b}$ for $\vec{b}$ or $-\vec{c}$ for $\vec{c}$. What is the greatest distance you can be from base camp at the end of the third displacement? (We are not concerned about the direction.)

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: In an orienteering class, you have the goal of moving as far (straight-line distance) from base camp as possible by making three straight-line moves. You may use the following displacements in any order: (a) $\vec{a}, 2.0 \mathrm{~km}$ due east (directly toward the east); (b) $\vec{b}, 2.0 \mathrm{~km} 30^{\circ}$ north of east (at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ toward the north from due east); (c) $\vec{c}, 1.0 \mathrm{~km}$ due west. Alternatively, you may substitute either $-\vec{b}$ for $\vec{b}$ or $-\vec{c}$ for $\vec{c}$. What is the greatest distance you can be from base camp at the end of the third displacement? (We are not concerned about the direction.)
A. 4.2 m
B. 4.6 km
C. 3.8 km
D. 2.9 km
E. 5.2 km
F. 3.5 m
G. 4.8 m
H. 5.0 km
I. 5.6 m
J. 3.2 km
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The three moves must be the vectors  

\[
\vec a = (2,0)\text{ km},\qquad 
\vec b = (2\cos30^\circ , 2\sin30^\circ) = (1.732,1)\text{ km},
\qquad 
\vec c = (-1,0)\text{ km},
\]

or the opposites \(-\vec b\) and \(-\vec c\).  
Hence the final displacement is  

\[
\vec S = \vec a + s_b\vec b + t\vec c,
\]

where \(s_b = \pm 1\) (choose \(\vec b\) or \(-\vec b\)) and \(t = \pm 1\) (choose \(\vec c\) or \(-\vec c\)).  

Writing components,

\[
S_x = 2 + s_b(1.732) - t,\qquad 
S_y = s_b(1).
\]

The magnitude is  

\[
|\vec S| = \sqrt{(2 + 1.732 s_b - t)^2 + 1^2}.
\]

Testing the four possibilities:

| \(s_b\) | \(t\) | \(S_x\) (km) | \(|\vec S|\) (km) |
|--------|------|--------------|-------------------|
| \(+1\) | \(+1\) | \(2.732\) | \( \sqrt{2.732^2+1}=2.94\) |
| \(+1\) | \(-1\) | \(4.732\) | \( \sqrt{4.732^2+1}=4.84\) |
| \(-1\) | \(+1\) | \(-0.732\) | \( \sqrt{0.732^2+1}=1.25\) |
| \(-1\) | \(-1\) | \(1.268\) | \( \sqrt{1.268^2+1}=1.62\) |

The largest distance occurs for \(s_b = +1\) (use \(\vec b\)) and \(t = -1\) (use \(-\vec c\)), giving  

\[
|\vec S| \approx 4.84\ \text{km}.
\]

Among the answer choices, this corresponds to **4.8 km**.

**Answer: the answer is (G).**

doc.question:
Assume all gases are perfect unless stated otherwise. Unless otherwise stated, thermodynamic data are for 298.15 K.  Given the reactions (1) and (2) below, determine $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} U^{\ominus}$ for reaction (3).

(1) $\mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{g})$, $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} H^{\ominus}=-184.62 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

(2) $2 \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$, $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} H^\ominus=-483.64 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

(3) $4 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{g})+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$

resps:
The reverse of reaction (1) (multiplied by 2) gives  

\[
4\;\mathrm{HCl(g)} \;\rightarrow\; 2\;\mathrm{H_2(g)} + 2\;\mathrm{Cl_2(g)}\qquad \Delta U = +2(184.62)=+369.24\;\text{kJ}
\]

For reaction (2) we convert the given \(\Delta H^\ominus\) to \(\Delta U^\ominus\):

\[
\Delta n = 2-3 = -1,\qquad 
\Delta U = \Delta H - \Delta nRT = -483.64\;\text{kJ} -(-1)(8.3145\times10^{-3}\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1})(298.15\;\text{K})
\]
\[
\Delta U = -483.64\;\text{kJ} + 2.48\;\text{kJ}= -481.16\;\text{kJ}
\]

Adding the two steps cancels the \(2\;\mathrm{H_2(g)}\) and yields reaction (3):

\[
4\;\mathrm{HCl(g)} + \mathrm{O_2(g)} \rightarrow 2\;\mathrm{Cl_2(g)} + 2\;\mathrm{H_2O(g)}
\]

\[
\Delta U^\ominus_{(3)} = 369.24\;\text{kJ} + (-481.16\;\text{kJ}) = -111.92\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1}
\]

Thus the correct choice is  

**H. -111.92 kJ mol⁻¹**.

doc.question: A 30-year-old nulliparous female presents to the office with the complaint of mood changes. She says that for the past several months she has been anxious, hyperactive, and unable to sleep 3 to 4 days prior to the onset of menses. She further reports that on the day her menses begins she becomes acutely depressed, anorectic, irritable, and lethargic. She has no psychiatric history. Physical examination findings are normal. She and her husband have been trying to conceive for over 2 years. History reveals a tuboplasty approximately 1 year ago to correct a closed fallopian tube. The most likely diagnosis is

She further reports that on the day her menses begins she becomes acutely depressed, anorectic, irritable, and lethargic. She has no psychiatric history. Physical examination findings are normal. She and her husband have been trying to conceive for over 2 years. History reveals a tuboplasty approximately 1 year ago to correct a closed fallopian tube. The most likely diagnosis is
A. Major depressive disorder
B. Seasonal affective disorder
C. cyclothymic personality
D. generalized anxiety disorder
E. adjustment disorder with depressed mood

Answer:", "type": "text"}]

The presentation describes cyclic mood symptoms that occur in the luteal phase (anxiety, hyperactivity, insomnia) and then abruptly shift to depressive symptoms with the onset of menses. This pattern is characteristic of premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD).

doc.question: The mean ionic activity coefficient of an electrolyte in a dilute solution is given approximately by ln \gamma_\pm = - A\surd(m) where m is the molality and A is a constant. Using the Gibbs-Duhem equation find the activity coefficient \gamma_1 of the solvent in this dilute solution in terms of A, m, and constants characteristic of the solvent and solute. Ob-serve that ln X_1 = ln [(n_1) / (n_1 + mv)] = ln [(1) / {(1 + mv) / n_1}] \approx - [(mv) / (n_1)] where v is the number of ions in the formula (for example v = 2 for NaCl) and n_1 is the number of moles of solvent in 1000 g of solvent. All quantities except \gamma_1 , the un-known, can thus be expressed in terms of the variable m.

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: The mean ionic activity coefficient of an electrolyte in a dilute solution is given approximately by ln \gamma_\pm = - A\surd(m) where m is the molality and A is a constant. Using the Gibbs-Duhem equation find the activity coefficient \gamma_1 of the solvent in this dilute solution in terms of A, m, and constants characteristic of the solvent and solute. Ob-serve that ln X_1 = ln [(n_1) / (n_1 + mv)] = ln [(1) / {(1 + mv) / n_1}] \approx - [(mv) / (n_1)] where v is the number of ions in the formula (for example v = 2 for NaCl) and n_1 is the number of moles of solvent in 1000 g of solvent. All quantities except \gamma_1 , the un-known, can thus be expressed in terms of the variable m.
A. \gamma_1 = e[{(Av^2) / (3n_1)} m^3/2]
B. \gamma_1 = e[{(Av^2) / (2n_1)} m^3/2]
C. \gamma_1 = e[{(Av) / (3n_1)} m^3/2]
D. \gamma_1 = e[{(Av) / (3n_1^2)} m^3/2]
E. \gamma_1 = [{(Av) / (3n_1)} m^3/2]
F. \gamma_1 = [{(Av^2) / (3n_1)} m^3/2]
G. \gamma_1 = [{(A) / (n_1v)} m^3/2]
H. \gamma_1 = e[{(A) / (2n_1v^2)} m^3/2]
I. \gamma_1 = e[{(A) / (3n_1v)} m^3/2]
J. \gamma_1 = [{(Av) / (2n_1)} m^3/2]
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
Using the Gibbs‑Duhem relation  

\[
\sum_i x_i\,d\ln \gamma_i =0
\]

for the solvent (1) and the \(v\) ions of the electrolyte, and assuming that each ion has the same activity coefficient \(\gamma_{\pm}\),

\[
x_1\,d\ln\gamma_1 + \frac{v n_2}{n_1+v n_2}\,d\ln\gamma_{\pm}=0 .
\]

Since  

\[
x_1=\frac{n_1}{n_1+v n_2},
\qquad 
\frac{v n_2}{n_1+v n_2}\Big/ x_1 =\frac{v n_2}{n_1},
\]

we obtain  

\[
d\ln\gamma_1 = -\frac{v n_2}{n_1}\,d\ln\gamma_{\pm}.
\]

In a dilute solution \(n_2=m\) (molality) and \(n_1\) (moles of solvent in 1000 g) is constant.  
Given the Debye–Hückel limiting law  

\[
\ln\gamma_{\pm}= -A\sqrt{m},
\]

\[
d\ln\gamma_{\pm}= -\frac{A}{2}m^{-1/2}\,dm .
\]

Hence  

\[
\ln\gamma_1 = -\frac{v}{n_1}\int m\left(-\frac{A}{2}m^{-1/2}\right)dm
            = \frac{vA}{2n_1}\int m^{1/2}dm
            = \frac{vA}{2n_1}\cdot\frac{2}{3}m^{3/2}
            = \frac{Av}{3n_1}\,m^{3/2}.
\]

Therefore  

\[
\boxed{\displaystyle \gamma_1 = \exp\!\left[\frac{Av}{3n_1}\,m^{3/2}\right]}
\]

which corresponds to choice **C**.  

the answer is (C)

doc.question: For point-to-point communication at higher frequencies, the desiredradiation pattern is a single narrow lobe or beam. To obtainsuch a characteristic (at least approximately) a multi-elementlinear array is usually used. An array is linear whenthe elements of the ar-ray are spaced equally along a straightline. In a uniform linear array the elements are fed withcurrents of equal magnitude and having a uniform progressivephase shift along the line. The pattern of such anarray can be obtained by addingvectoriallythe field strengthsdue to each of the elements. For a uniform array of non-directionalelements the field strength would be E_T = E_0 \vert1 +e^J\psi+ e^J2\psi + e^J3\psi + ... +e^j^(^n-1)\psi \vert(1) where \psi =\betadcos\textphi + \alpha and\alpha is the progressive phase shift between elements, (\alpha is theangle by which the current in any element leads the currentin the preceding element.) Compute the pattern of such a linear array.

arg_0:
[{"role": "system", "content": "For multiple choice questions, end your response with "the answer is (X)" where X is the letter.", "type": "text"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Question: For point-to-point communication at higher frequencies, the desiredradiation pattern is a single narrow lobe or beam. To obtainsuch a characteristic (at least approximately) a multi-elementlinear array is usually used. An array is linear whenthe elements of the ar-ray are spaced equally along a straightline. In a uniform linear array the elements are fed withcurrents of equal magnitude and having a uniform progressivephase shift along the line. The pattern of such anarray can be obtained by addingvectoriallythe field strengthsdue to each of the elements. For a uniform array of non-directionalelements the field strength would be E_T = E_0 \vert1 +e^J\psi+ e^J2\psi + e^J3\psi + ... +e^j^(^n-1)\psi \vert(1) where \psi =\betadcos\textphi + \alpha and\alpha is the progressive phase shift between elements, (\alpha is theangle by which the current in any element leads the currentin the preceding element.) Compute the pattern of such a linear array.
A. 0.512
B. 0.612
C. 0.212
D. 0.212 (with a different phase shift)
E. 0.412
F. 0.312
G. 0.112
H. 0.712
I. 0.912
J. 0.112 (with a different element spacing)
Answer:", "type": "text"}]

resps:
The array factor (normalized pattern) of a uniform linear array of \(n\) elements is  

\[
\boxed{ \displaystyle 
\frac{E_T}{E_0}= \Bigl|\,1+e^{j\psi}+e^{j2\psi}+\dots+e^{j(n-1)\psi}\Bigr|
            = \Bigl|\frac{\sin\!\bigl(\tfrac{n\psi}{2}\bigr)}
                         {\sin\!\bigl(\tfrac{\psi}{2}\bigr)}\Bigr|
}
\]

where  

\[
\psi = \beta d\cos\phi + \alpha .
\]

Thus the pattern is given by the magnitude of the sine‑ratio expression above.  

the answer is (A)

doc.question: A 16-year-old girl is brought to the office by her mother because she is concerned that she may have contracted meningitis from her soccer teammate, who was diagnosed with meningococcal meningitis and admitted to the hospital yesterday. The patient's soccer team traveled to a neighboring state to participate in a tournament 1 week ago and she shared a hotel room with the girl who was hospitalized. The patient feels well but is concerned she may have "caught the same bug." Medical history is remarkable for asthma. Medications include inhaled albuterol. Vital signs are temperature 37.2°C (98.9°F), pulse 64/min, respirations 16/min, and blood pressure 107/58 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate intervention for this patient at this time?
choices: No intervention is needed at this time

The appropriate management for an asymptomatic close contact of a patient with meningococcal meningitis is chemoprophylaxis with rifampin (or an alternative such as ciprofloxacin or ceftriaxone). Therefore, the most appropriate intervention is:

**I. Prescribe rifampin**  

the answer is (I)

doc.question: A 15-year-old girl is brought to the physician 3 months after she had a blood pressure of 150/95 mm Hg at a routine examination prior to participation in school sports. She is asymptomatic and has no history of serious illness. Twelve months ago, she was diagnosed with a urinary tract infection and treated with oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. She currently takes no medications. Subsequent blood pressure measurements on three separate occasions since the last visit have been: 155/94 mm Hg, 145/90 mm Hg, and 150/92 mm Hg. She is at the 50th percentile for height and 95th percentile for weight. Her blood pressure today is 150/90 mm Hg confirmed by a second measurement, pulse is 80/min, and respirations are 12/min. Examination shows no other abnormalities. Her hematocrit is 40%. Urinalysis is within normal limits. Cardiac and renal ultrasonography shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

She tells you that she moved away from home more than a year ago and that her parents contribute nothing to her support. She has a 6-month-old child and lives with a 28-year-old female cousin. doc.question: A supplier of ink for printers sent the following letter to all of its customers:"Closeout special! We have decided to no longer stock green ink cartridges. We have on hand a limited supply of green ink cartridges for all printers; when they're gone, they're gone! Please submit your orders as soon as possible to make sure your order can be filled. "One of the regular customers of the supplier sent the following reply by fax:"Sorry to hear that you will no longer carry green ink cartridges, since that is one of our favorite colors. Please ship 100 green ink cartridges to our office as soon as possible. "The supplier faxed an acknowledgement of the order to the customer with a promise that the cartridges would be shipped out in one week. The next day, the supplier received the following e-mail from the customer:"Please cancel our order. We just discovered that we already have plenty of green ink cartridges in inventory. " The supplier proceeded to sell its entire stock of green ink cartridges at its asking price to other customers. In an action for breach of contract by the supplier against the customer, what is the maximum amount of damages that the supplier should be entitled to recover?
choices: Consequential damages, since the green ink cartridges were unique because they were the last of their kind to be offered for sale by the supplier.
The cost of the ink cartridges plus any loss in profit from the potential sale to other customers.
$10,000, which is double the asking price of the cartridges, as a penalty for the customer's late cancellation.
Only incidental damages, if any, that the supplier has incurred in preparing the green ink cartridges for shipment to the customer before receiving the customer's e-mail.
The cost of the ink cartridges plus the cost of shipping, as the supplier had already promised to ship them out.
$5,000, which was the asking price for the 100 green ink cartridges ordered.
The cost of the ink cartridges plus any loss in profit from the sale to other customers, since the supplier had to sell the cartridges at a lower price.
The full cost of the cartridges plus any additional costs incurred in the sale to other customers.
Any additional costs incurred by the supplier in obtaining replacement cartridges to fulfill the customer's order.

doc.question: A nephew inherited a large parcel of unimproved land from his uncle. In need of cash, the nephew decided to sell the parcel. He contacted a real estate agent in the area about listing the parcel for sale. The nephew and the agent entered into a valid written contract whereby the agent promised to undertake best efforts to find a buyer for the parcel. The contract also provided that the agent was to be paid a commission of 5 percent on the gross sale price following the consummation of the sale and transfer of title. The agent succeeded in finding a buyer for the parcel. The agent notified the nephew that he had found a developer who wanted to purchase the parcel for $500,000. The agent handed the nephew a real estate sales contract, signed by the developer, in which the developer agreed to pay $500,000 for the purchase of the parcel. The nephew then signed the agreement himself. However, before consummation of the sale and transfer of title, the developer, without cause, repudiated the contract. Despite the agent's insistence, the nephew refused to either sue the developer to enforce the land sale contract or pay the agent his commission. If the agent sues the nephew for breach of the brokerage agreement, which of the following, if anything, is the agent's proper measure of recovery?
choices: Nothing, because the nephew did not breach the brokerage agreement.

$25,000, or the commission equivalent of 5 percent on the sale of the property for $500,000, because all conditions precedent to the nephew's duty to pay the commission were substantially fulfilled when the nephew and the developer entered into the land sale contract.
$10,000, as a partial commission due to the agent's work done before the sale was cancelled.
$12,500, or half the commission, because the sale reached the halfway point when the nephew and the developer signed the contract.
Nothing, because as a third-party beneficiary of the contract between the nephew and the developer, the agent can enforce the contract only against the developer, but not against the nephew.
$25,000, or the commission equivalent of 5 percent on the sale of the property for$500,000, because fulfillment of the consummation condition was prevented by an event beyond the agent's control.
$25,000, because the agent provided a buyer and thus fulfilled his part of the contract.
Nothing, because the sale did not go through and hence no commission is due.
Nothing, because the consummation condition has not been fulfilled, and the nephew's refusal to sue the developer will not excuse that condition.
$25,000, because the agent upheld his end of the contract in making his best efforts to find a buyer for the parcel.

doc.question:
This question refers to the following information.
"The conscience of the people, in a time of grave national problems, has called into being a new party, born of the nation's sense of justice. We of the Progressive party here dedicate ourselves to the fulfillment of the duty laid upon us by our fathers to maintain the government of the people, by the people and for the people whose foundations they laid. We hold with Thomas Jefferson and Abraham Lincoln that the people are the masters of their Constitution, to fulfill its purposes and to safeguard it from those who, by perversion of its intent, would convert it into an instrument of injustice. In accordance with the needs of each generation the people must use their sovereign powers to establish and maintain equal opportunity and industrial justice, to secure which this Government was founded and without which no republic can endure.
"This country belongs to the people who inhabit it. Its resources, its business, its institutions and its laws should be utilized, maintained or altered in whatever manner will best promote the general interest. It is time to set the public welfare in the first place."
Progressive Party Platform, 1912

choices: without which no republic can endure
establish and maintain equal opportunity and industrial justice
the people are the masters of their Constitution
Its resources, its business, its institutions and its laws should be utilized, maintained or altered
set the public welfare in the first place
the people must use their sovereign powers
the duty laid upon us by our fathers
